title: Module 4 quiz: Memory, context, and continuity

questions.prompt: Your assistant signs off 'enjoy your vacation!' months after the user mentioned a trip. What's wrong?

choices.text: Working memory has no expiration on temporal facts. Time-bound preferences (out-of-office, this-quarter goals, current project) need short TTLs (1-2 weeks default). Without aging, working memory drifts from reality.

questions.explanation: Temporal facts age. Durable identity preferences ride longer. The line is type — anything that smells time-bound defaults to short TTL.

questions.prompt: What's the three-layer memory architecture for assistants?

choices.text: Profile (structured, eager-loaded), episodic memory (RAG-style, lazy-retrieved), lessons learned (curated user-confirmed notes). Working memory bridges short-term and long-term. Each layer has different retrieval, expiration, and trust patterns.

questions.explanation: Three layers serve different purposes. Profile is small and always-on. Episodic is RAG-shaped. Lessons learned are curated. Each gets richer with use; each has its own update and expiration policy.

questions.prompt: Assistant retrieves from 8 sources for every query. Latency is 4-6s; cost high. Best fix?

choices.text: Add source routing — rule-based or small classifier picks 2-3 relevant sources per query. Latency drops to 1-2s; cost drops 50-70%. Calendar queries don't need Slack; Slack queries don't need user profile.

questions.explanation: Multi-source retrieval needs routing to scale. Pick relevant sources per query, retrieve top-K from each, rerank to 5-10 final chunks.

questions.prompt: User deleted 'VIP: Sarah' from memory; assistant re-inferred and re-added within days. User complains. Fix?

choices.text: Tell user the assistant is right.

Add a block list for previously-deleted entries. Combined with inference dampening (re-add requires strong signal + user confirmation), deleted facts stay deleted unless the user changes their mind. Honor the deletion intent.

questions.explanation: Shadow-profile problems break user trust. Block lists + confirmation gates on re-adding deleted facts respect the user's intent.

questions.prompt: Why build deletion plumbing early in an assistant product?

choices.text: GDPR/CCPA require it; deletion must include derived data (embeddings, summaries); retrofitting under regulatory pressure is painful; incident response sometimes needs fast specific removal. Build as first-class capability from v1 with documented fast-track flow.

questions.explanation: Real deletion is hard to retrofit. Privacy law, user requests, and incident response all need it. Build the plumbing — storage, derived data, indexes, audit logs — from v1 with a documented process and fast-track flow.
